examples.title: Cache Read Operation (Direct Mapping)
examples.description: Algorithm to check cache for data and handle misses
examples.code:
PROCEDURE CacheRead(address)
  offset = address MOD blockSize
  index = (address / blockSize) MOD numLines
  tag = address / (blockSize * numLines)
  
  IF valid[index] == TRUE AND tag[index] == tag THEN
    RETURN cacheData[index][offset]
  ELSE
    blockAddress = (address / blockSize) * blockSize
    mainMemory[blockAddress...blockAddress+blockSize-1] => cacheData[index]
    tag[index] = tag
    valid[index] = TRUE
    dirty[index] = FALSE
    RETURN cacheData[index][offset]
  ENDIF
ENDPROC
examples.explanation: This algorithm shows how a CPU reads data from a direct-mapped cache. It extracts offset and index from the address, checks if the requested block is in cache (tag match and valid bit set), returns data on hit, or fetches from main memory on miss.

examples.title: LRU Cache Replacement Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of Least Recently Used replacement policy using an ordered dictionary
examples.explanation: This implements an LRU cache using Python's OrderedDict. When capacity is reached, the least recently used item (first item) is evicted. move_to_end() updates the access order.

examples.title: Fully Associative Cache Lookup
examples.description: C implementation showing tag comparison across all cache lines
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates fully associative cache lookup where ALL cache lines must be searched (compared) to find a matching tag. This requires hardware comparators for each line, making it expensive but flexible - any block can go anywhere.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of cache lines

examples.title: Write-Through vs Write-Back Cache Simulation
examples.description: Java class demonstrating both write policies with dirty bit tracking
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates the difference between write-through and write-back policies. Write-through updates both cache and memory immediately (dirty stays false). Write-back only updates cache and sets dirty=true; main memory is only updated when the block is evicted.
